explicit_text: I was born in 1960 and grew up in Long Beach, California, but my family moved to Wilmington in 1942, which is part of the greater Long Beach harbor area. I consider Wilmington my lifelong home, right in the heart of the port town. Growing up in the sixties in this area shaped a lot of my experiences, especially being part of a union family connected to the local industries.
implicit_text: I was born in 1960 and grew up in a coastal city in Southern California, but my family moved to a nearby harbor community in 1942, which is part of the greater port area. I consider that harbor town my lifelong home, right in the heart of the shipping industry. explicit_text: My family moved to Wilmington in 1942, and I've lived there my entire life. Wilmington is the heart of the harbor and the port town, and it’s where I grew up and still live today. It’s also where my father’s ironworking company was based and where I started my union career.

explicit_text: My seven-year-old sister was diagnosed with juvenile rheumatoid arthritis when I was born in 1960. She suffered with this disease for 53 years until she passed away last year. Taking care of her shaped who I am today, as I helped with everything from feeding and bathing to pushing her wheelchair and driving her to California State University, Dominguez Hills.
implicit_text: My seven-year-old sister was diagnosed with a chronic autoimmune disease affecting joints when I was born in 1960. She suffered with this disease for 53 years until she passed away last year. Taking care of her shaped who I am today, as I helped with everything from feeding and bathing to pushing her wheelchair and driving her to a local state university.

explicit_text: My sister attended California State University, Dominguez Hills, where she earned her master's degree despite her physical challenges. I often drove her there because she used an electric cart to get around campus. It was inspiring to see her pursue higher education even with juvenile rheumatoid arthritis.

explicit_text: My father and brothers worked as ironworkers on major projects including the Vincent Thomas Bridge. Growing up in Wilmington, I was proud to see my family contribute to such iconic structures in the harbor area. Their work on the bridge was part of a long tradition of ironworking in our family.

explicit_text: Along with the Vincent Thomas Bridge, my father and brothers also helped build the Gerald Desmond Bridge. entity: Mary Star of the Sea High School
explicit_text: I attended Mary Star of the Sea High School, a Catholic school located in San Pedro, California. It was where I completed my high school education before starting my first union job. The school was part of my early life in the harbor area.
implicit_text: I attended a Catholic high school located in a nearby coastal community known for its maritime heritage. It was where I completed my high school education before starting my first union job. The school was part of my early life in the harbor area.

explicit_text: At 18, I joined the Retail Clerks International Union, Union 770, in Harbor City. This was my first union membership, and it was a big deal because I was working part-time at a grocery store and wanted a better-paying, organized labor job. I stayed with the union for a couple of years while attending college.

explicit_text: I attended Harbor College in Wilmington after high school, balancing college classes with my work as a retail clerk. It was during this time that I considered becoming an accountant but eventually stayed connected to union work. Harbor College was an important step in my education and career path.
implicit_text: I attended a community college in the harbor community after high school, balancing college classes with my work as a retail clerk. explicit_text: My father came to California from Nebraska in 1938. He initially worked in the oil fields and briefly on the docks before becoming an ironworker and starting his own company. Nebraska was where his journey began before settling in Wilmington.
implicit_text: My father came to California from a Midwestern state in 1938. He initially worked in the oil fields and briefly on the docks before becoming an ironworker and starting his own company. That state was where his journey began before settling in the harbor town.

explicit_text: When I first joined the ironworkers union, I was placed at the Champlin Refining and Chemicals Company in Wilmington, which is now located in Vallejo. I spent my first year there mostly in the boneyard, mastering the torch and making shims. It was a tough start, especially as the only woman on site among thousands of men.

explicit_text: A World War II veteran was the steward who supported me during my early days as an ironworker at Champlin Refinery. He would have lunch with me every day and share stories from the war, which helped me feel connected and respected despite being the only woman on the job. His presence was a meaningful part of my experience.

His presence was a meaningful part of my experience.

explicit_text: My foreman once asked if I had ever been on the E ride at Disneyland, and then he had me lifted in a basket made by ironworkers up to the top of a building. This was my first experience in the raising gang, working alongside tall, experienced ironworkers. The Disneyland reference made the moment memorable and helped me feel part of the team.

explicit_text: My father had a big ironworking yard down by Local 13 in Wilmington, where we eventually worked together welding. Local 13 was central to our family's ironworking legacy, and it was special to share that experience with my dad. It was a place where I grew into my role as an ironworker and earned respect.

explicit_text: To become an ironworker, I attended an apprentice program at Cerritos College for three years, taking classes two nights a week while working full time. This college education was part of my journey to becoming the first female ironworker in Southern California and Nevada. It was a demanding but rewarding experience.

explicit_text: I grew up in a family of ironworkers; my father owned A-1 Erection and worked on major projects like the Vincent Thomas and Gerald Desmond Bridges. In 1981, I became the first female ironworker in Southern California and Nevada, facing challenges but earning respect through hard work and skill. Being an ironworker was a proud and defining part of my life.

explicit_text: When I started working on the docks as a clerk, I often rode on forklifts driven by Teamsters, learning the ropes of the waterfront. The Teamsters were part of the dockside culture, and I remember sitting with one driver my first day, not knowing the proper protocol. They were key players in the harbor labor scene I grew up around.

explicit_text: The Golden State Metal Trades Council was involved in the union activities I participated in, including the longshore union. I recall receiving court papers related to the Golden suit, which was part of the legal and organizational framework for union workers at the harbor. This council was part of the broader labor environment that shaped my career.
